title: The Person and Work of Christ, Part 9
speaker: Elder Nick Anderson
source_files: 6–9-24 - Person and Work of Chist - Part 9 - Elder Nick Anderson.txt

outline.title: Introduction: Why Christ's Intercession Is Often Overlooked
subpoints: We rightly focus on the cross, death, and resurrection, but Christ's work did not end there — He continues to work for us now
Three problems: (1) we overlook intercession; (2) we misunderstand it — we picture Christ idly waiting; (3) we underappreciate it
Personal testimony: John Owen's 'Death of Death in the Death of Christ' convinced the speaker of definite atonement specifically through Owen's emphasis on Christ's present intercession

If we overlook this doctrine we miss: boldness in prayer, all the benefits Christ merited, peace of conscience, assurance of eternal life, forgiveness of daily sin, accepted prayers, and a guard against trusting our own merits

outline.title: What Is Intercession? (WLC Q. 55)
subpoints: Definition (Louis Berkhof): Intercession is when one is called in for aid — an advocate, one who pleads the cause of another and gives wise counsel

Q. 55: Christ makes intercession by His appearing in our nature continually before the Father in heaven, in the merit of His obedience and sacrifice on earth, declaring His will to have it applied to all believers, answering all accusations against them, and procuring for them quiet of conscience, access with boldness to the throne of grace, and acceptance of their persons and services
Hebrews 7:25: 'He is also able to save to the uttermost those who come to God through Him, since He always lives to make intercession for them'
Romans 8:34: 'Who is he who condemns? It is Christ who died, and furthermore is also risen, who is even at the right hand of God, who also makes intercession for us'

outline.title: For Whom Does Christ Intercede?
subpoints: Christ only intercedes for the elect — those who have true faith in Christ, and those who will have true faith
John 10:16: 'Other sheep I have which are not of this fold; them also I must bring, and they will hear My voice'
John 17:9: 'I pray for them. I do not pray for the world, but for those whom You have given Me, for they are Yours'
John 17:20: 'I do not pray for these alone, but also for those who will believe in Me through their word'
Implication: Christ's intercession never fails; it is unthinkable that Christ should intercede for specific people and any of them end up in hell
Either Christ died and interceded for everyone and fails (which is impossible), or He died and intercedes for the elect, every single one of whom will be saved

outline.title: What Does Christ's Intercession Consist Of?
subpoints: Christ's intercession consists of two inseparable things: sacrifice and prayer
Romans 8:34: 'It is Christ who died... who also makes intercession for us' — His death and intercession cannot be separated
On earth: He prayed for His disciples; He prayed on the cross for those who crucified Him (Luke 23:34); He rendered obedience and made propitiation
In heaven: He continually presents Himself as the perfect sacrifice before the Father (Hebrews 9:24-25) and continually prays for His people
Hebrews 7:25: 'He always lives to make intercession for them' — this does not end; right now, even as we speak, Christ is interceding for His people

outline.title: How Does Christ Make Intercession? (The Details of WLC Q. 55)
subpoints: By appearing in our nature continually before the Father: He had to be a real man to fulfill righteousness as a man; He is now in heaven as the God-man, fulfilling His high priestly office

In the merit of His obedience and sacrifice: all the conditions of salvation have been met in Christ; we don't merit anything; our weak and sometimes insincere prayers are made acceptable by His intercession
By declaring His will to have His merits applied to all believers: John 17:9, 20, 24 — He prays for their sanctification, their presence with Him, and their beholding His glory
By answering all accusations: Romans 8:33-34 — 'Who shall bring a charge against God's elect? It is God who justifies' — no one can bring a valid charge; not even ourselves
By procuring quiet of conscience: we sin daily, yet no one can condemn us; Romans 5:1-2 — 'Having been justified by faith, we have peace with God'
By giving believers access with boldness to the throne of grace: Hebrews 4:16 — 'Let us therefore come boldly to the throne of grace, that we may obtain mercy'
By securing acceptance of persons and services: Ephesians 1:6 — 'He made us accepted in the Beloved'; even our tainted services are accepted through Christ's intercession

outline.title: Application: What This Means for Our Faith and Prayer
subpoints: If Christ is interceding for you, you will always and unfailingly have everything you will ever need in this life and the life to come — if you don't have something, you don't need it right now
Our prayers are weak, sometimes insincere, sometimes distracted — but Christ's intercession makes them acceptable; this should give us great confidence
Do not shy away from examining your heart, discovering idols, confessing sins — the only one you harm by not doing this is yourself and everyone around you
Even if you have not prayed or read your Bible all year, Christ is praying for you — let that be an incentive, not an excuse
We are not accepted because we are great people or because we prayed every day — Christ alone makes us accepted; we are accepted because we are united to Him by faith

summary: This is Part 9 of the Person and Work of Christ Sunday school series, covering Westminster Larger Catechism Question 55 on Christ's intercession. Elder Nick Anderson opens with a personal testimony: while in college, reading John Owen's 'Death of Death in the Death of Christ,' it was Owen's emphasis on Christ's present intercession — specifically Hebrews 7:25 — that first convinced him of definite atonement, before any of the other TULIP points. He argues that Christ's intercession is often overlooked, misunderstood (as though Christ is idly waiting), and underappreciated, resulting in a weakened faith. He defines intercession using Berkhof's definition — an advocate who pleads the cause of another — and then works through WLC Q. 55's description of how Christ makes intercession: by appearing in our nature before the Father, in the merit of His obedience and sacrifice, declaring His will for His people, answering all accusations, procuring quiet of conscience, granting access with boldness, and securing the acceptance of both persons and services. A significant section addresses for whom Christ intercedes: the elect only, as shown in John 17 where Jesus explicitly says He does not pray for the world. The implication is that Christ's intercession cannot fail, which means every person He intercedes for will certainly be saved. The session closes with practical applications: God's people will have everything they need, weak and imperfect prayers are made acceptable by Christ's intercession, and believers should come boldly to the throne of grace regardless of their failures, since Christ is always their advocate.
transcript:
## Introduction: Why We Miss Christ's Intercession

As we are continuing our series on the person and work of Christ, today we are going to look at Christ's intercession for us. I know I have talked about this before, but I thought I would talk about it a little more today, because the doctrine of Christ's intercession is perhaps overlooked, misunderstood, and in my experience, underappreciated.

When we talk about Christ's atonement — His time here on earth, His death, His resurrection — we put a lot of focus on the cross, on His death and what He accomplished in that, and His resurrection. Rightfully so. But I think sometimes we forget that His work did not end right there on the cross. When He rose from the dead, He continues to work for us. Sometimes we forget to consider that He is still doing stuff for us now, and we miss out because of that. Our faith is weakened because we forget that Christ still works for us.

Sometimes I think we also misunderstand what Christ is doing when He intercedes for us. We hear that Christ is sitting at the right hand of the Father, and at least in my mind, sometimes I picture Him just sitting around, twiddling His thumbs, waiting for the day when He is going to return and bring us to glory. But He is not inactive.

Here is a personal story. I grew up in an evangelical church that preached the gospel. But it was not a Reformed church. When I went to college, there was an ongoing debate on campus: free will, predestination, does God choose us? I read Ephesians 1 and thought — of course He predestines us. But it was not really clear in my mind how all of that worked.

I read a book by J.I. Packer called The Quest for Godliness, about the Puritans. He mentioned John Owen's Death of Death in the Death of Christ. I ended up reading that book. It was extremely difficult to read — whole paragraphs in one sentence, many of which I just skipped. But in that book, Owen makes the case against universal atonement. I could see that he was going somewhere; I could see that the atonement was limited in a certain sense; I could see that free will is an idol. But when it came to Christ's intercession — the points Owen made specifically about Christ interceding for the people He died for — that turned me into a Calvinist. It was so clear that Christ was interceding for His people and no one else. Most people who become five-point Calvinists do not start with limited atonement. That was the first point I was convinced of, because of John Owen's emphasis on what Christ is doing now in heaven.

If we miss this doctrine, we miss: boldness in prayer, all the benefits Christ merited, peace of conscience, assurance of eternal life, forgiveness of daily sins, accepted prayers, and protection against trusting our own merits.

## What Is Intercession? (WLC Q. 55)

The Larger Catechism in Question 55 asks: How does Christ make intercession? The answer: Christ makes intercession by His appearing in our nature continually before the Father in heaven, in the merit of His obedience and sacrifice on earth, declaring His will to have it applied to all believers, answering all accusations against them, and procuring for them quiet of conscience, notwithstanding daily failings, access with boldness to the throne of grace, and acceptance of their persons and services.

The catechism does a great job describing Christ's intercession, but does not give us a precise definition. Louis Berkhof's definition: intercession is when one is called in for aid — an advocate, one who pleads the cause of another and gives wise counsel.

John 17:17: "Sanctify them by Your truth. Your word is truth." Christ is praying His high priestly prayer, pleading with the Father on our behalf and giving us wise counsel by asking the Lord to sanctify us by His Word.

Hebrews 7:25: "Therefore He is also able to save to the uttermost those who come to God through Him, since He always lives to make intercession for them." This was the verse Owen anchored his arguments on. Because Christ is interceding for us, He is able to save us completely and finally.

Romans 8:34: "Who is he who condemns? It is Christ who died, and furthermore is also risen, who is even at the right hand of God, who also makes intercession for us." Paul asks who is going to condemn us — the answer is no one, because Christ is interceding for us.

1 John 2:1: "My little children, these things I write to you, so that you may not sin. And if anyone sins, we have an Advocate with the Father, Jesus Christ the righteous." Sin cannot keep us from God because we have Christ Himself as our Advocate.

## For Whom Does Christ Intercede?

Christ only intercedes for the elect — those who have true faith in Christ and those who will have true faith in Christ.

John 10:16: "Other sheep I have which are not of this fold; them also I must bring, and they will hear My voice; and there will be one flock and one shepherd." Christ is speaking of His sheep versus those who are not His sheep.

John 17:9: "I pray for them. I do not pray for the world, but for those whom You have given Me, for they are Yours."

John 17:20: "I do not pray for these alone, but also for those who will believe in Me through their word."

John 17:24: "Father, I desire that they also whom You gave Me may be with Me where I am, that they may behold My glory."

This brings us to a very important point of application. If Christ is interceding for you, you will always and unfailingly have everything you will ever need in this life and in the life to come. And this is our first point of application.

Some may be thinking: I don't have this thing or that thing. I don't have a better job, or I'm sick and haven't been healed. But if you don't have those things, it means you don't need them right now. We should be careful how we use the word "need." If I don't have it, I don't need it right now. He may give it later. We are told to pray for the things we think we need, even the things we want if they accord with His word. But if we don't have something right now, it's because we don't need it right now, and we need to cling to Christ more.

And here is why Christ intercedes only for the elect: because Christ's intercession never fails. It is unthinkable to hold to a doctrine that says Christ's death and intercession is for all men and yet some of those men will end up in hell. If Christ intercedes for specific people, those people are going to heaven. Either Christ died and interceded for everyone and fails — which is unthinkable — or He died and intercedes for the elect, and every single one of them is going to heaven, because He cannot fail in His purpose.

## What Does Christ's Intercession Consist Of?

Christ's intercession consists of two things that cannot be separated: sacrifice and prayer. Romans 8:34: "It is Christ who died... who also makes intercession for us." His death and resurrection is the basis for His intercession. In the Old Testament, the high priest on the Day of Atonement would enter the Holy of Holies with the sacrifice, present it to God, and also offer prayers. In the same way Christ entered heaven and offered Himself as the perfect sacrifice on our behalf. But His intercession does not stop there — that is where sometimes in our thinking we stop.

On earth: He prayed for His disciples. He prayed on the cross for those who crucified Him: "Father, forgive them, for they do not know what they do" (Luke 23:34). I believe those people Christ prayed for became saved at some point, because Christ's intercession never fails.

In heaven: Hebrews 9:24-25: "For Christ has not entered the holy places made with hands, which are copies of the true, but into heaven itself, now to appear in the presence of God for us." He continually presents Himself as the perfect sacrifice before the Father and continually offers prayers for His people. And this does not end. Hebrews 7:25: He always lives to make intercession. Right now, even as I speak, Christ is interceding for you, if you are a true believer.

## How Does Christ Make Intercession?

First, He appears in our nature continually before the Father in heaven. Why does He appear in our nature? Because He had to fulfill righteousness as a real man. He had to be God because only God could bear the eternal wrath of the Father. He continually appears before the Father because this is part of His high priestly office that is not yet fulfilled, and He is doing it to give us full, free, total access to the Father when we pray, and to satisfy the Father's wrath against sin.

Second, He intercedes in the merit of His obedience and sacrifice on earth. All the conditions of salvation have been met in Christ. We don't merit anything. When you pray, is your mind ever distracted? Do you always pray sincerely? Our prayers are very weak. Sometimes I even wonder, as I'm praying, am I actually sincere? This is why Christ's intercession is so important — our prayers are accepted by God through His intercession, even when they are insincere. Everything we need has already been given to us. 2 Peter 1:3: "His divine power has given to us all things that pertain to life and godliness."

Third, He declares His will to have His merits applied to all believers. John 17:9, 20, 24. He prays for our sanctification, for our presence with Him, for our beholding His glory. Christ prays specifically for your sanctification. If He prays for your sanctification, that is a great motivation to get to work. We are not idle — sanctification involves our work. Philippians 2:12-13: "Work out your own salvation with fear and trembling. For it is God who works in you both to will and to do for His good pleasure."

Fourth, He answers all accusations against believers. Romans 8:33-34: "Who shall bring a charge against God's elect? It is God who justifies. Who is he who condemns? It is Christ who died." This must be one of the most precious promises in the entire Bible — because we sin every day, and who is going to bring a charge against us? No one. Not Satan, not other people, not even ourselves. We cannot bring a valid charge against ourselves because Christ is interceding for us.

Fifth, He procures quiet of conscience despite daily failings. Romans 5:1-2: "Having been justified by faith, we have peace with God through our Lord Jesus Christ, through whom also we have access by faith into this grace in which we stand and rejoice in hope of the glory of God." We stand. On judgment day, the sinners will not stand (Psalm 1), but believers will, because we are right with God. We rejoice in hope of the glory of God because of His life, death, resurrection, and intercession.

Sixth, He gives believers access with boldness to the throne of grace. Hebrews 4:16: "Let us therefore come boldly to the throne of grace, that we may obtain mercy and find grace to help in time of need." Note that it does not say "if" we are in need — it says "in time of need." There is an assumption that there will be times of need. In fact, all the time is the time when we are in need. Do not shy away from admitting your sins before the Lord. Do not shy away from examining your heart. If you are sitting there thinking, "I do not want to do that," then just pray that God will give you the will to do it. And He will.

Seventh, He secures acceptance of persons and services. Ephesians 1:6: "He made us accepted in the Beloved." We are not accepted because we are great people, not because we prayed every day or read our Bible through this year. Christ alone makes us accepted. Even if you have not prayed at all this month, you are still accepted because of what Christ did and continues to do in interceding for you.

Even our services — sometimes we do good things for the wrong reasons, or our motivations are tainted. But 1 Peter 2:5 says we are a holy priesthood offering up spiritual sacrifices acceptable to God through Jesus Christ. Even our in-adequate and sometimes sinful services are accepted through His intercession.

So do not let your failings stop you from coming before the throne of grace. Christ not only died for you — He continues to intercede for you to this day, to make you the kind of person the Lord wants you to be. It does not matter where you are in your sanctification. Christ is praying for you. That gives us great incentive to go to the throne of grace with boldness and ask the Lord to sanctify us more and more, because that is His will for us, and that is one of the great reasons why He intercedes.

Our gracious God, we thank you so much for Christ, for His ongoing and continual intercession for us. We thank you that you are so compassionate in giving us Christ, and so good to us, that He always lives to make intercession for us and is able to save us to the uttermost. We pray that you would bless us for the rest of this day, bless the service to come, work in our hearts, and be with us. In Christ's name. Amen.
